title: Superconducting triode effect in a quantum-dot Josephson junction with a biased top gate
authors: Yu-Hang Li, Xiaan Du, Hua Jiang, X. C. Xie
abstract: Non-reciprocal supercurrents enable non-dissipative rectification, holding great promise for superconducting electronics. Conventionally, this non-reciprocity, termed the superconducting diode effect, requires the simultaneous breaking of time-reversal and parity symmetries. Here, we propose a superconducting triode effect in an asymmetric quantum-dot Josephson junction coupled to an additional metallic top gate, which breaks the parity symmetry while explicitly preserving time-reversal symmetry. We demonstrate that the supercurrent across this junction exhibits a strong non-reciprocal effect that can be continuously manipulated via the top gate to achieve an ideal unidirectional supercurrent, thus manifesting a superconducting triode effect. Furthermore, under radio-frequency radiation, this junction exhibits highly asymmetric Shapiro steps, realizing fully quantized supercurrent rectification. Our work not only provides an alternative physical mechanism for the superconducting diode effect observed in Josephson junctions with explicit time-reversal symmetry, but also introduces a new tuning knob to manipulate supercurrent non-reciprocity.
summary: The paper introduces a novel 'superconducting triode effect' in a quantum-dot Josephson junction by incorporating a biased top gate. This setup allows the creation of strong supercurrent non-reciprocity—a key feature for superconducting diodes—while explicitly preserving time-reversal symmetry. The theoretical framework uses advanced Green's function techniques to predict tunable, quantized rectification effects.
main_problem: The research addresses how to achieve a robust Superconducting Diode Effect (SDE) in Josephson junctions while explicitly preserving Time-Reversal symmetry, which is conventionally thought to require breaking both T and P symmetries.
main_result: They propose a superconducting triode effect using an asymmetric quantum-dot junction with a top gate bias that allows for the continuous tuning of supercurrent non-reciprocity towards an ideal unidirectional flow while maintaining T-symmetry.
method: The analysis employs the Keldysh Non-Equilibrium Green's Function (NEGF) formalism, extending to Floquet theory to handle time-periodic driving from RF radiation.
model_or_system: The system is an asymmetric quantum-dot Josephson junction coupled to superconducting leads and an additional metallic top gate. The Hamiltonian describes the coupling between these three components.
key_observables: Current-Phase Relations (CPR), non-reciprocal supercurrent efficiency ($\eta$), and highly asymmetric Shapiro steps under RF irradiation.
important_parameters: Top gate bias voltage ($V$), superconducting gap ($\Delta_0$), and the ratio of coupling strengths ($t_L, t_R, t_M$).
main_assumptions: The theory is derived using approximations like the weak-link limit for initial calculations but claims universality across coupling regimes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate CPR asymmetry vs. gate bias (Fig. 2), non-reciprocal efficiency ($\eta$) mapping, and highly asymmetric Shapiro step distributions under combined DC/RF drive (Fig. 3).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical model and the goal of achieving T-symmetric SDE via a superconducting triode effect. It then calculates supercurrent components using NEGF formalism in both DC and AC regimes, culminating in demonstrations of tunable non-reciprocity and quantized rectification.
why_interesting: This work provides an alternative, symmetry-preserving mechanism for observing diode-like behavior in superconductors, which is highly relevant to superconducting electronics and quantum circuit design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic for the STE in an asymmetric quantum- dot Josephson junction with an additional biased top gate (N). Here, the coupling strength between the quantum-dot and the three leads are tL, tR, and tM, respectively. The green line in top gate illustrates the Andreev reflection under a bias V . The yellow arrow illustrates the tunneling super- current between left (L) and right (R) superconducting leads driven by the phase difference ϕ = ϕL −ϕR. The cyan arrows highlight the normal-metal-assisted injection currents, medi- ated by non-equilibrium Andreev processes involving the top gate (N).

FIG. 2. (a) and (b) Current-phase relations Is(ϕ) for a symmetric junction with tL = tR = 0.1 (a) and an asymmetric junction with tL = 0.2, tR = 0.1 (b) under different top gate bias V . Here, tM = 0.5. (c) Non-reciprocal supercurrent efficiency η on the V -δt plane. The red dashed line highlights the critical phase boundary with η = 1, which separates the normal non-reciprocal regime and the ideal regime that only allows the unidirectional supercurrent. The white dashed line highlights the cases in (a) and (b). Here, δt = tL−tR, tR = 0.1 and tM = 0.5.

FIG. 3. (a) Maximum I+ c (solid lines) and minimum I− c (dashed lines) critical supercurrents of Is(ϕ) as a function of the scaled radiation amplitude eVac/ℏω for different top gate bias V . (b) and (c) I-V curve for the junction under different top gates V in the absence [(a), Vac = 0] and presence [(b), eVac/ℏω = 0.05] of radiation. The green shadings in the background highlight the symmetric supercurrent at V = 0, serving as a benchmark. The inset displays five representative Shapiro step widths versus scaled radiation amplitude at V = 0. (d) Differential resistance dV/dI versus total time-averaging current Ia and scaled radiation amplitude eVac/ℏω at a representative top bias V = 0.05....
